title: F1 Mechanics, Kinematics, Energy, and Momentum
short: advanced secondary physics specialization for mechanics, kinematics, energy, and momentum
purpose: The learner models motion and interaction using force, energy, and momentum relationships without reducing mechanics to equation selection by surface cue only.
exit: explains motion using kinematic, force, energy, and momentum models in bounded cases
distinguishes when force, energy, or momentum reasoning is the better explanatory tool
connects representations, measurements, and model assumptions in mechanics tasks
succeeds across more than one advanced mechanics context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: coordinate verbal, graphical, and mathematical mechanics representations
explain why a given mechanics model applies in the bounded case
distinguish interaction reasoning from formula recall
use evidence from motion or collision data without overclaiming precision
failure: equation-cue substitution
learner selects formulas from surface cues without a coherent mechanics model.
force-energy conflation
learner merges force, work, and energy reasoning without distinction.
momentum-scope confusion
learner misapplies momentum ideas outside the bounded interaction context.
representation-fragmentation
learner cannot coordinate graphs, measurements, and symbolic reasoning.
interventions: representation-matching routines that require one mechanics situation to be explained across words, graphs, and equations
model-selection tasks comparing force, energy, and momentum explanations for the same event
collision and motion analyses that bound when momentum reasoning is justified
graph-to-measurement checks tying symbolic work back to observed motion
items: Why is choosing an equation by keyword not enough in a mechanics problem?
A strong answer explains the physical model, assumptions, and represented quantities rather than matching words to a formula only.
How is an energy explanation different from a force explanation in the same motion case?
A strong answer distinguishes interaction cause from system-state change and shows why each model highlights different features of the motion.
What makes a momentum claim well bounded?
It should identify the interaction interval, relevant system boundary, and what measurements support the claim.
noteSecure: Explains mechanics with bounded force, energy, and momentum models rather than equation hunting.
noteEmerging: Can perform some procedures but model selection and representation coordination remain unstable.

title: F2 Rotation, Oscillation, Gravitation, and Mechanical Systems
short: advanced secondary physics specialization for rotation, oscillation, gravitation, and mechanical systems
purpose: The learner extends mechanics into rotational, oscillatory, and gravitational systems without treating these topics as disconnected special-case formulas.
exit: explains rotational and oscillatory behavior using coherent mechanical-system reasoning
connects gravitation to orbital or field-based mechanics in bounded cases
distinguishes linear and rotational analogies without collapsing them into identical relations
succeeds across more than one advanced mechanics context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: use rotational variables and system constraints meaningfully
explain oscillation with restoring-effect reasoning instead of pattern description only
connect orbital motion to interaction models and system conditions
avoid overextending linear analogies without checking model fit
failure: rotation-linear collapse
learner treats rotational relations as identical to linear ones without bounded interpretation.
oscillation-pattern only
learner describes periodicity without explaining restoring dynamics.
gravitation-formula isolation
learner recalls gravitation formulas without model-based orbital reasoning.
mechanical-system overgeneralization
learner extends one mechanics model to incompatible rotational or orbital cases.
interventions: linear-versus-rotational comparison routines tied to physical interpretation
oscillation explanations that force restoring-effect and energy reasoning
orbit-model prompts connecting gravitation, motion, and system boundary conditions
case sorting that separates when rotational, oscillatory, or orbital models fit
items: Why is a rotational variable not just the linear one with a new symbol?
A strong answer explains what the rotational quantity represents and how the system geometry changes the interpretation.
What makes an oscillation explanation more than saying it repeats?
A strong explanation identifies the restoring effect, system conditions, and how energy changes within the oscillation.
Why does orbital motion need more than a gravitation formula quote?
It requires connecting interaction strength, motion, and system conditions rather than citing a relation without interpretation.
noteSecure: Explains rotational, oscillatory, and gravitational systems through coherent mechanical models.
noteEmerging: Recognizes topic labels but explanations remain formula-driven and weakly integrated.

title: F3 Fluids, Thermodynamics, and Thermal Systems
short: advanced secondary physics specialization for fluids, thermodynamics, and thermal systems
purpose: The learner explains pressure, flow, heat, and thermal-system behavior using physical models without reducing thermal systems to one-variable temperature talk.
exit: explains fluid and thermal behavior using pressure, energy, and system-condition reasoning
distinguishes heat, temperature, and energy-change claims in bounded cases
connects macroscopic thermal behavior to system models without overextending simplifications
succeeds across more than one fluids or thermodynamics context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: use pressure and flow concepts with bounded system interpretation
distinguish temperature descriptions from energy-transfer explanations
reason about thermal systems using state change, conservation, or process models
avoid one-variable explanations for multi-condition systems
failure: temperature-energy collapse
learner treats temperature and energy transfer as interchangeable.
pressure-flow shortcut
learner uses pressure or flow labels without system-level explanation.
thermal-state oversimplification
learner ignores important state or process conditions in thermal reasoning.
fluid-thermal context drift
learner applies one fluid or thermal relation outside its justified context.
interventions: temperature-versus-energy comparison tasks with explicit transfer reasoning
fluid-system diagrams that require boundary, pressure, and flow interpretation
thermal-process maps connecting state variables, transfer, and constraints
bounded-case sorts for fluid and thermodynamic models
items: Why is a temperature change not automatically the same as an energy explanation?
A strong answer distinguishes measured temperature from the processes and transfers that explain the system's energy change.
What makes a pressure explanation more than naming a formula?
It should identify the system, boundaries, and how pressure relates to force, flow, or depth in the bounded case.
How can a thermal model be overextended?
It can be overextended when it ignores relevant conditions, process assumptions, or the limits of the simplified relation being used.
noteSecure: Explains fluids and thermal systems with bounded pressure, transfer, and system-condition reasoning.
noteEmerging: Uses thermal and fluid vocabulary but explanations remain one-variable and formula-driven.

title: F4 Electrostatics, Circuits, and Magnetic Fields
short: advanced secondary physics specialization for electrostatics, circuits, and magnetic fields
purpose: The learner explains electric and magnetic phenomena using field, circuit, and interaction models without collapsing electricity into current-only or formula-only reasoning.
exit: explains electric interactions and fields using bounded system models
explains circuits through potential, current, resistance, and system relations
connects magnetic behavior to moving charge or field interaction models in bounded cases
succeeds across more than one advanced electricity and magnetism context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: distinguish charge, field, potential, current, and resistance roles appropriately
reason about circuits as systems rather than isolated component facts
connect magnetic effects to interaction models instead of memorized direction rules only
avoid overextending one electrical relation to all contexts
failure: current-only electricity collapse
learner explains all electric behavior through current without field or potential reasoning.
component-island circuits
learner describes circuit parts without system-level relation among them.
magnetism-rule memorization
learner uses direction rules without interaction-model explanation.
electromagnetic-context overgeneralization
learner applies one electric or magnetic relation beyond its bounded case.
interventions: field-potential-current comparison tasks that separate electrical roles
whole-circuit reasoning routines that require source-path-load explanations
magnetic interaction prompts tied to moving charge and field models
case sorting that distinguishes electrostatic, circuit, and magnetic contexts
items: Why is current alone not enough to explain a circuit outcome?
A strong answer also considers potential differences, resistance, and how the full circuit system is configured.
What makes a field explanation different from a component description?
A field explanation models how interactions are distributed in space rather than naming one object or part only.
Why is a magnetic direction rule not a complete explanation?
A complete explanation should connect the rule to the underlying charge or field interaction in the bounded case.
noteSecure: Explains electricity and magnetism through field and system reasoning rather than isolated rules.
noteEmerging: Uses electric and magnetic vocabulary but treats circuits and fields as fragmented procedures.

title: F5 Waves, Optics, Quantum, and Modern Physics
short: advanced secondary physics specialization for waves, optics, quantum, and modern physics
purpose: The learner explains optical, wave, and modern-physics phenomena using multiscale models without treating wave behavior and quantum ideas as disconnected curiosity topics.
exit: explains wave and optical behavior using coherent propagation and interaction models
connects light behavior to both wave and bounded modern-physics perspectives where appropriate
uses model choice carefully in quantum or modern-physics contexts
succeeds across more than one advanced wave or modern-physics context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: distinguish what wave models explain well in the bounded case
connect optical behavior to interaction, path, or representation choices
treat modern-physics models as explanatory tools with limits rather than literal pictures
avoid mixing incompatible model claims without justification
failure: wave-feature listing
learner names wave features without using a propagation or interaction model.
optics-ray-only collapse
learner uses one optics representation without checking whether it fits the case.
quantum-literalism
learner treats modern-physics models as literal pictures rather than bounded explanations.
model-mixing without-boundary
learner blends wave, ray, or quantum ideas without justification.
interventions: wave propagation tasks that require model-based explanation rather than feature recall
representation selection routines comparing ray, wave, and other optics models
model-limit discussions for quantum and modern-physics explanations
boundary checks that ask why one model fits a case better than another
items: Why is naming wavelength or frequency not enough to explain a wave case?
A strong answer uses those quantities inside a propagation or interaction model rather than listing them as isolated facts.
When can an optics representation mislead?
It can mislead when it is used outside the situations it was designed to explain or when another model better fits the phenomenon.
Why should a quantum model not be treated as a literal picture?
It is a bounded explanatory model that captures certain behaviors and constraints rather than a direct visual description of reality.
noteSecure: Explains waves, optics, and modern physics through bounded model choice and interaction reasoning.
noteEmerging: Recognizes wave and quantum vocabulary but mixes models without checking their limits.

title: F6 Calculus-Embedded Physics Modeling
short: advanced secondary physics specialization for calculus-embedded physics modeling
purpose: The learner integrates calculus-supported modeling into advanced mechanics and electricity-magnetism reasoning without treating mathematical procedure as a substitute for physical interpretation.
exit: uses calculus-supported reasoning to model change and accumulation in bounded physics cases
connects mathematical operations back to physical quantities, assumptions, and system meaning
uses advanced modeling to extend mechanics or electricity-magnetism explanations without overclaiming scope
succeeds across more than one calculus-embedded physics context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: interpret rate and accumulation mathematically and physically
state modeling assumptions and quantity meanings explicitly
coordinate advanced mathematical work with evidence and physics explanation
avoid treating symbolic completion as physical understanding
failure: calculus-procedure substitution
learner performs calculus steps without explaining the physics meaning of the quantities.
quantity-interpretation drift
learner loses track of what changing or accumulated quantities represent physically.
model-scope overreach
learner extends a successful mathematical model beyond the bounded physical case.
math-physics disconnect
learner cannot connect advanced symbolic work to system evidence or explanation.
interventions: rate-and-accumulation interpretation tasks tied directly to physics quantities
assumption audits requiring each mathematical step to be connected to a physical claim
bounded-case checks that ask where the calculus-supported model stops fitting
evidence-to-model routines linking derived relations back to observed system behavior
items: Why is a correct derivative or integral not enough in an advanced physics explanation?
A strong answer also explains what quantity is changing or accumulating and how that interpretation supports the physics model.
What makes a calculus-supported model overextended?
It becomes overextended when its assumptions or bounded physical conditions no longer hold, even if the mathematics still looks valid.
How should advanced symbolic work connect back to evidence?
It should be tied to measured quantities, system behavior, and the explanatory claim rather than presented as self-justifying mathematics.
noteSecure: Uses calculus-supported physics modeling with explicit quantity meaning, assumptions, and bounded interpretation.
noteEmerging: Can perform some advanced mathematics but does not reliably connect it back to physics meaning.
